heroOverview: Opisthorchiasis is a food-borne parasitic infection caused by the liver flukes Opisthorchis viverrini and Opisthorchis felineus, transmitted when people eat raw or undercooked freshwater fish containing infectious metacercariae. An estimated 9 to 10 million people are infected globally, concentrated in the Mekong River basin of Thailand, Laos, and Cambodia and in Siberia and Eastern Europe. The adult worms take up residence in the bile ducts and gallbladder, where low-grade chronic inflammation can persist for decades. Most acute infections cause only mild gastrointestinal symptoms or none at all, but long-standing opisthorchiasis with Opisthorchis viverrini is the strongest known risk factor for cholangiocarcinoma — bile duct cancer — dramatically elevating lifetime cancer risk in heavily endemic communities. Early diagnosis and treatment with a single dose of praziquantel is curative in most cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 9-10 million people infected worldwide; northeast Thailand alone accounts for ~8 million cases (WHO, 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 30-60 years in endemic riverine communities who consume raw fish dishes (e.g., koi pla in Thailand)
keyStats.avgAge: Peak prevalence in adults aged 30-50 years; infection accumulates over decades of repeated exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic in the Mekong sub-region (Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam) and in Russia, Ukraine, and Kazakhstan

definition: Opisthorchiasis (ICD-10: B66.0) is infection with trematodes (flukes) of the genus Opisthorchis, principally Opisthorchis viverrini in Southeast Asia and Opisthorchis felineus in Siberia and parts of Europe. The life cycle requires two intermediate hosts: freshwater snails of the genus Bithynia and cyprinid freshwater fish. Humans and other fish-eating mammals become definitive hosts by ingesting metacercariae in insufficiently heated, raw, or fermented fish. After ingestion, larvae excyst in the duodenum and migrate up the bile duct, maturing into hermaphroditic adults 5-10 mm long within 3-4 weeks. Adult worms reside in the intrahepatic bile ducts, common bile duct, and gallbladder and can survive for 10 to 25 years in the human host. Worm burden varies widely: light infections (fewer than 100 worms) are usually asymptomatic; heavy infections (thousands of worms in highly endemic areas) cause chronic cholangitis, biliary fibrosis, and a three- to five-fold increase in the risk of cholangiocarcinoma — a bile duct cancer with poor prognosis. Opisthorchiasis is classified as a neglected tropical disease by the WHO and is the fourth most common food-borne parasitic disease globally. The infection falls under infectious disease and gastroenterology/hepatology for chronic management.

typesClassification.description: Occurs 3-4 weeks after initial heavy infection, causing fever, right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, diarrhoea, and eosinophilia. More common in non-immune travellers entering endemic areas than in lifelong residents who acquire small worm burdens gradually.
Fewer than 100 worms; the most common form in endemic populations. Usually asymptomatic or producing mild intermittent dyspepsia, fatigue, and abdominal discomfort for years to decades.

Hundreds to thousands of worms causing persistent cholangitis, cholestasis, biliary fibrosis, and significantly elevated risk of cholangiocarcinoma and gallbladder cancer. Found in long-term residents of highly endemic areas consuming raw fish repeatedly.

The most feared complication; bile duct carcinoma that develops in a subset of patients with long-standing heavy infection, typically in the fifth to seventh decade. O. viverrini has been classified as a Group 1 carcinogen (IARC) specifically because of this association.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant abdominal pain or discomfort, often described as a dull ache under the right rib margin, from bile duct inflammation and distension.
Intermittent jaundice — yellowing of the skin and eyes — occurring when biliary obstruction impairs bilirubin excretion, particularly during acute exacerbations.
Dyspepsia including nausea, bloating, and early satiety, related to impaired bile flow and concurrent gallbladder dysfunction.
Diarrhoea or loose stools, more prominent during acute infection or exacerbations, reflecting altered intestinal motility and biliary dysfunction.
Fatigue and general malaise, which can be profound in heavy infections and persist even between symptomatic episodes.
Hepatomegaly — an enlarged, sometimes tender liver — palpable on physical examination in moderate to heavy infections.
Fever and chills during episodes of acute cholangitis, when bacterial secondary infection complicates biliary obstruction.
Anorexia and unintentional weight loss in chronic heavy infections, reflecting malabsorption and a catabolic state from persistent infection.
Eosinophilia — elevated eosinophils on blood count — particularly prominent during the tissue-migration phase of acute infection.
Urticaria (itchy skin rash) and allergic symptoms during larval migration in primary infection as the immune system responds to parasite antigens.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild, intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort in a person who regularly eats raw or undercooked freshwater fish in an endemic region.
Unexplained eosinophilia on a routine blood count in a patient from or having visited Southeast Asia or Siberia.
Mild indigestion and bloating persisting for months without a clear dietary explanation in someone from an endemic area.
Vague fatigue in an otherwise healthy adult from northeast Thailand or the Mekong sub-region without another identified cause.
emergencySigns: Fever with jaundice and right upper quadrant pain — Charcot's triad of acute cholangitis, which requires urgent biliary decompression and antibiotics.
Sudden severe abdominal pain with distension suggesting gallbladder perforation or acute pancreatitis as biliary complications.
Rapidly progressive jaundice with dark urine and pale stools indicating biliary obstruction that may require endoscopic or surgical intervention.
New back pain, dramatic weight loss, or obstructive jaundice in a person with known chronic opisthorchiasis — features that raise concern for cholangiocarcinoma and need urgent imaging.
High fever with altered mental status in a patient with biliary disease, suggesting ascending cholangitis with sepsis.

causes.cause: Ingestion of raw or undercooked freshwater fish
causes.description: The exclusive route of human infection. Metacercariae encysted in the flesh and skin of cyprinid fish (carp family) survive smoking, light salting, and fermentation unless temperatures above 60°C for at least several seconds are sustained. Traditional dishes such as koi pla (raw fish salad) in northeast Thailand are the primary vehicle in the highest-burden areas.

causes.cause: Opisthorchis viverrini lifecycle in the Mekong basin
causes.description: O. viverrini eggs passed in human or animal faeces contaminate freshwater, hatch into miracidia that infect Bithynia snails, develop into cercariae, then penetrate cyprinid fish to form metacercariae. Inadequate sanitation and continued raw fish consumption sustain the transmission cycle in endemic communities.

causes.cause: Opisthorchis felineus in Siberia and Europe
causes.description: O. felineus follows the same lifecycle using different Bithynia snail species and an overlapping range of freshwater fish. Prevalence exceeds 50% in some Siberian communities where salted and dried raw fish are dietary staples.

Opisthorchis eggs are highly resistant in moist environments and survive in water for weeks to months. Open defaecation near waterways and the use of human waste as fish pond fertiliser maintain high environmental egg loads in endemic villages.

Repeated worm migration, egg deposition, and secreted excretory-secretory products drive periductal fibrosis and chronic inflammation of the bile duct epithelium. This creates a sustained genotoxic and proliferative environment that, over 10 to 30 years, can transform into cholangiocarcinoma — the mechanism underlying IARC's Group 1 carcinogen classification of O. viverrini.

riskFactors.factor: Consumption of raw or fermented freshwater fish
riskFactors.description: The single most important and modifiable risk factor; eliminating raw freshwater fish ingestion eliminates the risk of opisthorchiasis entirely. Cultural food preferences in the Mekong region and Siberia make this a challenging behaviour to change, even with health education.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic areas
riskFactors.description: The Mekong River basin (northeastern Thailand, Lao PDR, Cambodia, southern Vietnam) and Siberia/Eastern Europe carry the highest transmission intensity. Travellers or immigrants from these regions carry ongoing risk.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 30 years with repeated exposure
riskFactors.description: Worm burden accumulates over decades of repeated raw fish consumption; older adults in highly endemic communities carry the heaviest infections and consequently the greatest cholangiocarcinoma risk.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex in most endemic studies
riskFactors.description: Most Southeast Asian studies find prevalence about 1.5- to 2-fold higher in men than women, potentially reflecting differences in raw fish consumption frequency and occupation-related exposures.

riskFactors.factor: Poor access to sanitation and clean water
riskFactors.description: Open defaecation and the practice of using human or animal waste to fertilise fish ponds maintain high environmental contamination and sustain the snail and fish intermediate hosts.

Combined opisthorchiasis and heavy alcohol consumption has been associated with a synergistically elevated risk of cholangiocarcinoma in Thai cohort studies, suggesting alcohol amplifies the carcinogenic effect of chronic biliary inflammation.
Re-infection after treatment is common in endemic settings due to continued fish consumption habits; each re-infection adds cumulative biliary damage and escalates cancer risk over the life course.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30-60 years living in Mekong River basin communities who eat koi pla or similar raw fish dishes
Siberian and Eastern European communities in whom salted, dried, or pickled river fish are dietary staples
Men slightly more than women in most endemic-area prevalence surveys
Rural agricultural communities with limited sanitation and high fish-farming practices
International travellers and immigrants from endemic regions who continue traditional food practices
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of opisthorchiasis requires a combination of clinical assessment, epidemiological exposure history, laboratory tests, and imaging. Clinicians should ask specifically about recent or habitual consumption of raw or undercooked freshwater fish and about geographic origin or travel to endemic areas. Stool examination for parasite eggs is the most widely used diagnostic method in endemic settings: Opisthorchis eggs are small (27-30 µm), operculated (lidded), and embryonated, but distinguishing them from those of Clonorchis sinensis on morphology alone is difficult. Formalin-ether concentration techniques or the Kato-Katz method improve sensitivity. A single stool examination has only 55-60% sensitivity for light infections; examining three consecutive specimens raises sensitivity to approximately 80-90%. A specific Opisthorchis stool antigen enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is more sensitive than egg detection for light infections and is increasingly used in research settings. Serology (antibody detection by ELISA) supports diagnosis particularly in travellers with acute infections and negative stool tests, though cross-reaction with other trematode infections reduces specificity. In chronic disease with biliary complications, abdominal ultrasound demonstrates bile duct dilatation, gallbladder wall thickening, and sludge. CT or MRI cholangiography better characterises biliary strictures and tumour masses. Blood tests typically show peripheral eosinophilia during the acute phase, elevated alkaline phosphatase and gamma-GT in chronic biliary disease, and conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia when obstruction is present. When cholangiocarcinoma is suspected, CA 19-9 and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) serum levels are checked alongside cross-sectional imaging, though these markers have limited specificity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects operculated Opisthorchis eggs in stool; the primary field-applicable diagnostic test in endemic settings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a fresh stool sample in a labelled container; the laboratory prepares a thick smear and examines it under a microscope. Testing three separate stools on different days maximises sensitivity to around 80-90%.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects parasite-derived antigens directly in stool, useful in light infections where egg shedding is irregular and egg detection is insensitive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same stool sample is sent to a reference laboratory; results typically available within 1-2 days. More sensitive than egg microscopy for low worm burdens.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects host antibodies against Opisthorchis antigens, supporting diagnosis particularly in travellers and in acute infection before egg shedding begins.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw is sent to a reference or specialised laboratory. Cross-reactions with Clonorchis and other trematodes can occur; results are interpreted together with clinical history.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses biliary tract anatomy — bile duct dilatation, gallbladder wall thickening, intraluminal sludge — and screens for mass lesions suggesting cholangiocarcinoma in chronic infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless gel-on-skin scan taking 15-20 minutes; no radiation. Standard monitoring tool recommended every 6-12 months in heavily infected patients in endemic areas.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with eosinophil differential
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects peripheral eosinophilia (often 10-40% of white cells) characteristic of acute helminth infection and useful as a screening prompt in travellers returning from endemic regions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available in 1-2 hours. Eosinophilia resolves after successful treatment, making it a useful treatment-response marker.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides detailed imaging of the biliary tree in complicated disease — biliary strictures, intrahepatic stones, or mass lesions suspicious for cholangiocarcinoma — guiding therapeutic planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRCP is non-invasive (MRI scan with 30-60 minutes duration); ERCP involves a flexible scope passed through the mouth under sedation, and can be therapeutic as well as diagnostic.

treatmentOverview: Praziquantel is the drug of choice for opisthorchiasis and is highly effective in eradicating worm burden. The standard regimen is a single dose of 40 mg/kg (or 25 mg/kg in some protocols) taken orally with food, achieving cure rates of approximately 65-95% in clinical trials; a repeat dose at 2 weeks increases efficacy in heavy infections. Side effects are generally mild and transient: dizziness, nausea, headache, and abdominal discomfort lasting 1-6 hours, which is partly because dying worms release antigens that provoke a systemic reaction. Albendazole at 400 mg twice daily for 7 days has been used as an alternative with somewhat lower efficacy (approximately 40-60% cure) when praziquantel is unavailable. Tribendimidine, a drug used in China, showed promising efficacy in trials in Lao PDR with a favourable side-effect profile, but it is not yet widely available outside Asia. After treatment, egg count should be checked by stool examination 4-6 weeks later to confirm parasitological cure; re-treatment is given if eggs persist. In endemic settings, treatment must be accompanied by dietary counselling to prevent re-infection from continued raw fish consumption, since re-infection rates of 50-70% within 12 months have been documented in the absence of behaviour change. Biliary complications — cholangitis, cholecystitis, pancreatitis — require standard medical or surgical management in addition to antiparasitic therapy. Patients with chronic heavy infection should undergo regular ultrasound surveillance for cholangiocarcinoma, particularly if aged over 40 years. Mass drug administration programmes with praziquantel are used in high-endemicity communities as a public health control strategy.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel (single dose)
medicalTreatments.description: A pyrazinoisoquinoline anthelmintic that causes spastic paralysis and tegumental disruption in trematodes; given as a single oral dose of 40 mg/kg with food. Worms are expelled or absorbed over the following days. Repeated courses at 25 mg/kg three times in one day are used for very heavy infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure rates of 65-95% in clinical trials; repeated dose raises cure to approximately 95% in most settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole (7-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: A benzimidazole anthelmintic given at 400 mg twice daily for 7 days as an alternative when praziquantel is unavailable or contraindicated. Mechanism involves disrupting microtubule function in the fluke.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40-60% cure rate in opisthorchiasis, lower than praziquantel; useful as a second-line option or in co-infected individuals also carrying soil-transmitted helminths.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for secondary bacterial cholangitis
medicalTreatments.description: When bacterial superinfection complicates biliary obstruction (acute cholangitis), broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negative organisms — such as ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, or piperacillin-tazobactam — are added to antiparasitic therapy and biliary drainage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard antibiotic regimens achieve clinical improvement in approximately 80-90% of acute cholangitis episodes when combined with adequate biliary decompression.

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (adjunctive, chronic disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as an adjunct in patients with biliary sludge and mild cholestasis from chronic opisthorchiasis to improve bile flow, reduce biliary inflammation, and delay fibrosis progression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in liver enzyme elevations and biliary sludge in small studies; not a replacement for antiparasitic treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Tribendimidine (investigational)
medicalTreatments.description: A dehydroaminoacetaldehyde anthelmintic with demonstrated efficacy against liver flukes in controlled trials in Lao PDR and China. Given as a single oral dose with a favourable tolerability profile compared to praziquantel.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 60-80% cure rates in Phase 2/3 trials for opisthorchiasis; not yet widely licensed outside Asia.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) with stenting
surgicalOptions.description: Used to relieve biliary obstruction from strictures, stones, or tumour in patients with complicated chronic opisthorchiasis or when cholangiocarcinoma is suspected. Can be both diagnostic and therapeutic.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success rate for biliary stenting in experienced centres exceeds 90%; biliary decompression achieved in approximately 85-90% of benign stricture cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the gallbladder in patients with recurrent cholecystitis, gallstones, or a calcified gallbladder related to chronic opisthorchiasis-driven inflammation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy has a complication rate below 3% in elective cases; resolves recurrent cholecystitis in approximately 95% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatic resection or biliary reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when cholangiocarcinoma is confirmed and resectable — typically involving partial hepatectomy and bile duct excision with hepaticojejunostomy reconstruction. The only potentially curative treatment for biliary cancer.
surgicalOptions.successRate: R0 (complete) resection rates of 50-75% in specialised centres; 5-year survival approximately 30-40% after curative resection, dropping to less than 10% when unresectable.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary modification (cooking fish thoroughly)
alternativeTreatments.description: Cooking freshwater fish to an internal temperature of 60°C for at least 1 minute kills metacercariae entirely. Freezing at -20°C for 7 days also eliminates infectivity. This is the most effective non-pharmacological prevention and treatment-support measure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mass drug administration (community praziquantel)
alternativeTreatments.description: WHO-endorsed public health strategy in hyperendemic communities: providing praziquantel annually or biannually to all residents reduces community worm burden, though re-infection is common without concurrent behaviour change.

whySeeSpecialist: Most uncomplicated opisthorchiasis is managed by primary care or infectious disease physicians with a single praziquantel dose and follow-up stool testing. A gastroenterologist or hepatologist should be involved when biliary complications arise — cholangitis, biliary strictures, gallstones, or suspected cholangiocarcinoma. Patients over 40 with a history of heavy chronic infection and long-term residence in an endemic area warrant specialist-led surveillance ultrasound for bile duct cancer, given the five- to tenfold elevated lifetime risk. Oncology referral is needed promptly if cholangiocarcinoma is identified, since early-stage disease offers the only chance of curative resection.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic countries, public health facilities and district hospitals offer praziquantel treatment. For residents of non-endemic countries, seek a travel medicine or infectious disease specialist familiar with tropical parasitic infections. For biliary complications, a hepatologist or gastroenterologist with access to ERCP and biliary imaging is appropriate. Cholangiocarcinoma requires a multidisciplinary team including hepatobiliary surgery, oncology, and radiology at a specialist centre — outcomes are significantly better at high-volume liver cancer centres than at general hospitals.
preventionStrategies: Cook freshwater fish thoroughly to an internal temperature of at least 60°C for 1 minute — frying, boiling, or baking to this standard kills all metacercariae.
Freeze fish at -20°C for at least 7 days before consumption if raw preparation is intended.
Avoid raw, undercooked, or fermented freshwater fish dishes in or from endemic areas, including koi pla (Thailand/Laos) and similar preparations.
Improve sanitation in endemic communities: use latrines rather than defaecating near waterways; avoid using human waste to fertilise fish ponds.
Support and participate in local mass drug administration programmes in endemic areas.
Undergo periodic stool testing and ultrasound surveillance if you live in or have returned from an endemic area with a history of consuming raw freshwater fish, especially if over 40 years old.
lifestyleModifications: Permanently avoid raw or undercooked freshwater fish after treatment to prevent re-infection.
Adopt cooking methods (frying, baking, steaming) that reliably reach 60°C throughout the fish.
Reduce alcohol consumption, particularly if you have a history of heavy opisthorchiasis, as alcohol and chronic biliary inflammation act synergistically in cholangiocarcinoma risk.
Attend all follow-up appointments for stool testing 4-6 weeks after treatment to confirm cure.
Arrange annual liver ultrasound if you had heavy or long-duration infection and are over 40 years old.

recommended: Fully cooked freshwater fish at temperatures above 60°C — safe and nutritious once metacercariae are killed
Marine (saltwater) fish and shellfish not implicated in Opisthorchis transmission
A balanced diet with adequate fibre to support gut health
Adequate hydration to support bile flow during and after treatment
avoid: Raw, fermented, or lightly pickled freshwater fish in or from endemic regions
Koi pla, lap pla, and other traditional raw fish preparations common in northeast Thailand and Laos
Dried raw river fish from Siberia or Eastern Europe prepared without sufficient temperature treatment
Excessive alcohol, which compounds liver and biliary damage in chronic infection

exerciseGuidelines: There are no specific exercise restrictions for opisthorchiasis. Regular moderate physical activity is encouraged and supports general immune function. During an episode of acute cholangitis — with fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain — rest and medical treatment take priority. After successful praziquantel treatment and confirmed parasitological cure, there is no limitation on physical activity.
dailyManagement: Take praziquantel with food to reduce nausea and improve absorption; rest for the first 24 hours as dizziness is common.
Return for stool examination 4-6 weeks after treatment to confirm egg clearance; report persistent symptoms.
In endemic settings: commit to eating only fully cooked freshwater fish from the day of treatment onward.
Keep a record of when you were treated for future reference, since re-infection is indistinguishable from treatment failure on stool testing.
Arrange annual abdominal ultrasound if you are over 40, had heavy chronic infection, or lived in a hyperendemic area for more than 10 years.
prognosis: The prognosis of uncomplicated opisthorchiasis treated promptly with praziquantel is excellent: parasitological cure is achieved in approximately 65-95% of patients after one or two doses, symptoms resolve within weeks, and eosinophilia normalises within 1-3 months. Re-infection is the main challenge in endemic communities, where re-infection rates of 50-70% within 12 months have been documented when raw fish consumption continues. The long-term outlook is substantially worse for patients with heavy chronic infection: opisthorchiasis from O. viverrini is a Group 1 carcinogen (IARC), and long-term residents of hyperendemic areas carry a two- to threefold elevated risk of hepatobiliary cancer. Even after antiparasitic treatment, existing fibrotic and preneoplastic biliary changes may not fully reverse, though treatment earlier in life reduces cumulative cancer risk. Cholangiocarcinoma carries a 5-year survival below 10% when unresectable, making early detection through surveillance ultrasound a life-saving intervention. Overall, opisthorchiasis is curable with available medication when caught before biliary complications develop, but the disease burden in highly endemic communities remains enormous because of the difficulty of changing deeply embedded dietary traditions.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms of acute opisthorchiasis typically resolve within 2 to 4 weeks of successful treatment. Eosinophilia normalises within 1 to 3 months. A follow-up stool test at 4-6 weeks confirms parasitological cure. In chronic disease with biliary changes, liver enzyme elevations and ultrasound abnormalities may improve over 3 to 12 months after eradication of worm burden, but established biliary fibrosis does not fully reverse. Biliary complications such as cholangitis require separate treatment and longer recovery periods depending on severity.
complications: Ascending cholangitis — bacterial superinfection of obstructed bile ducts; presents with Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, right upper quadrant pain) and can progress to sepsis without urgent treatment.

Cholangiocarcinoma — bile duct cancer in long-standing heavy infection; the most feared complication, responsible for approximately 89% of cholangiocarcinoma cases in northeast Thailand where O. viverrini is endemic.
Cholelithiasis and cholecystitis — gallstone formation and gallbladder inflammation driven by biliary stasis and chronic inflammation.
Pancreatitis — bile duct obstruction by worms or stones can trigger pancreatic enzyme activation, causing acute or chronic pancreatitis.
Biliary fibrosis and cirrhosis — periductal fibrosis from chronic inflammation can progress to portal hypertension in very heavy long-standing infections.
Hepatomegaly and hepatic dysfunction — in heavy infections, persistent inflammation and obstruction impair liver function over years.

supportResources.name: CDC — Opisthorchiasis
supportResources.description: U.S. CDC diagnostic and treatment guidance for opisthorchiasis, including clinical information for healthcare providers.

supportResources.name: Lao Tropical and Public Health Institute — Liver Fluke Control
supportResources.description: WHO programme information on neglected tropical disease control, including the liver fluke control programme in the Mekong region.

sources.title: Sripa B et al. Liver Fluke Induces Cholangiocarcinoma. PLOS Medicine, 2007
Keiser J, Utzinger J. Food-borne Trematodiases. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2009

confusedWithConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Clonorchis sinensis and Opisthorchis species are morphologically almost identical and share the same transmission route; geographic origin is the key distinguishing feature — clonorchiasis predominates in East Asia (China, Japan, Korea), opisthorchiasis in Southeast Asia and Siberia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fascioliasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fasciola hepatica is a larger liver fluke (25-30 mm) acquired from watercress and aquatic plants, not fish; it causes an acute febrile illness with pronounced eosinophilia, and the bile duct location and treatment (triclabendazole, not praziquantel) differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial cholangitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary bacterial cholangitis presents similarly to complicated opisthorchiasis with fever, jaundice, and biliary pain, but lacks the raw fish exposure history and parasite eggs in stool; opisthorchiasis is often the underlying cause of secondary bacterial cholangitis in endemic areas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis B also causes hepatomegaly, jaundice, and liver enzyme elevation but is a viral infection transmitted by blood or sexual contact, not food-borne, and is identified by serological testing rather than stool examination.

relatedConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Clonorchis sinensis causes an almost identical clinical syndrome via the same fish-borne transmission route and is co-endemic with O. viverrini in some regions of East and Southeast Asia.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another food-borne liver fluke infection with overlapping biliary pathology, though acquired from plants rather than fish and requiring a different drug (triclabendazole).

relatedConditions.name: Cholangitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic opisthorchiasis is one of the most common causes of recurrent pyogenic cholangitis in Southeast Asia; the two conditions frequently coexist and require combined management.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major water-related parasitic infection common in the same endemic regions; both are neglected tropical diseases treated with praziquantel and linked to hepatic complications.

faqs.question: What is opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Opisthorchiasis is a parasitic infection of the liver's bile ducts caused by the liver flukes Opisthorchis viverrini or O. felineus. It is transmitted by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish and is common in Southeast Asia, Siberia, and Eastern Europe.

faqs.question: How do you get opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: You get opisthorchiasis by eating raw, undercooked, or fermented freshwater fish containing encysted larvae. Once swallowed, the larvae migrate into the bile ducts and mature into adult flukes. Drinking water or touching an infected person does not transmit it.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Many people have no symptoms, especially with light infections. When symptoms occur they include right-sided abdominal pain, indigestion, nausea, fatigue, and intermittent jaundice. Fever and severe abdominal pain develop if bile duct infection becomes complicated.

faqs.question: How is opisthorchiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The main test is stool examination for characteristic small operculated eggs, ideally from three separate samples to improve sensitivity. Blood tests showing elevated eosinophils support the diagnosis. Abdominal ultrasound assesses biliary complications, and serological tests can help in light or early infections.

faqs.question: How is opisthorchiasis treated?
faqs.answer: A single dose of praziquantel (40 mg/kg) cures most infections, with cure rates of 65-95%. A second dose two weeks later is given for heavy infections. Side effects — dizziness, nausea, mild abdominal pain — are usually brief. Stool re-testing at 4-6 weeks confirms cure.

faqs.question: Can opisthorchiasis cause cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Long-standing heavy infection with Opisthorchis viverrini is a proven cause of cholangiocarcinoma (bile duct cancer), classified as Group 1 carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer. People over 40 with a history of heavy infection should have regular ultrasound surveillance.

faqs.question: Where is opisthorchiasis most common?
faqs.answer: Opisthorchiasis is most common in northeast Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam (O. viverrini) and in Russia, Ukraine, and Kazakhstan (O. felineus). An estimated 9-10 million people worldwide are infected, predominantly in rural riverine communities.

faqs.question: How long do the worms survive in the body?
faqs.answer: Adult Opisthorchis flukes can survive in the human bile ducts for 10 to 25 years without treatment, continuously laying eggs and causing low-grade biliary inflammation. This is why cumulative damage and cancer risk increase with age in endemic areas.

faqs.question: Can opisthorchiasis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, by cooking freshwater fish to at least 60°C throughout, or by freezing at -20°C for 7 days before consumption. Avoiding raw fish dishes in endemic areas is the most reliable prevention. Improved sanitation and not defaecating near waterways also break the transmission cycle.

faqs.question: Is praziquantel safe for opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel is generally safe and well tolerated. Common temporary side effects include dizziness, headache, nausea, and abdominal discomfort for a few hours after the dose, partly from dying worm antigen release. Serious adverse effects are rare. It is avoided in the first trimester of pregnancy unless the risk of disease is very high.

faqs.question: Can you get opisthorchiasis more than once?
faqs.answer: Yes. Treatment clears the current worm burden, but re-infection occurs rapidly if raw fish consumption continues. Studies in endemic areas found re-infection rates of 50-70% within 12 months after successful treatment. Changing dietary habits is essential to prevent repeated infections.

faqs.question: What is the difference between opisthorchiasis and clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Both are liver fluke infections with almost identical biology and clinical presentation. Opisthorchis species are found in Southeast Asia and Siberia; Clonorchis sinensis is the dominant species in East Asia (China, Korea, Japan). Under a microscope the eggs are nearly identical, and treatment with praziquantel is the same.

faqs.question: Does opisthorchiasis affect the liver?
faqs.answer: Yes. Flukes live in the bile ducts inside the liver and the gallbladder, causing inflammation, bile duct thickening, and eventually fibrosis. Liver enzymes including alkaline phosphatase and GGT are commonly elevated, and the liver may be enlarged and tender on examination.

faqs.question: How long does it take to develop symptoms after eating contaminated fish?
faqs.answer: Acute symptoms can develop 3-4 weeks after a heavy initial infection as the larvae migrate and mature. In people who regularly eat small amounts of contaminated fish, worm burden accumulates slowly over years, and symptoms typically appear only when the burden becomes heavy — often a decade or more after regular exposure began.

faqs.question: What ultrasound findings suggest opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound in opisthorchiasis may show dilated intrahepatic bile ducts, a thickened gallbladder wall, biliary sludge, and echogenic material within ducts. In chronic disease, periductal fibrosis creates a characteristic echogenic rim around bile ducts. Mass lesions raise concern for cholangiocarcinoma.

faqs.question: Can opisthorchiasis cause pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, though this is less common than biliary complications. Flukes or fluke-related biliary stones can obstruct the pancreatic duct, triggering acute or chronic pancreatitis. Patients with unexplained recurrent pancreatitis from an endemic region should be tested for liver fluke infection.

faqs.question: Should children in endemic areas be tested for opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children in endemic communities often acquire low-worm-burden infections early in life from eating fish dishes prepared for adults. Screening with stool examination and treating infected children with age-appropriate praziquantel dosing reduces cumulative biliary damage and long-term cancer risk.

faqs.question: Can cooking or freezing fish make it safe to eat in endemic areas?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cooking freshwater fish to an internal temperature of 60°C for at least 1 minute kills all metacercariae. Freezing at -20°C for 7 days is also effective. These measures make freshwater fish completely safe and do not require avoiding fish consumption altogether.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine against opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: No vaccine is currently available for opisthorchiasis. Research into subunit vaccines targeting fluke excretory-secretory antigens is ongoing, but prevention remains based on cooking fish thoroughly, improving sanitation, and periodic treatment with praziquantel in endemic communities.

faqs.question: What happens if opisthorchiasis is left untreated for many years?
faqs.answer: Untreated chronic heavy infection leads to progressive biliary fibrosis, recurrent cholangitis, gallstone formation, and substantially elevated risk of cholangiocarcinoma. After 10-20 years of repeated heavy infection, some patients develop bile duct cancer, which carries a poor prognosis when detected late.

regionalNames.name: Bai pii maeng (ใบพี่แมง)

metaDescription: Opisthorchiasis is a liver fluke infection from raw freshwater fish causing bile duct disease and cancer risk. Learn symptoms and praziquantel treatment.
